tasks.title: Address context-rotting static files
tasks.notes: decisions.md, session-history.md, pickup.md rot between sessions — replace with maintainable alternatives. EXTENDED: The vault project view currently stores static agent-written files (project-state.md, decisions.md, session-history.md, pickup.md). These should NOT be stored documents — they should be generated on-demand from live sources of truth: tasks.json, git log, watcher.db, session branches. Solution: GET /api/project/{name}/context endpoint that assembles a fresh snapshot at query time. Permanent artifacts (case studies, design docs, real work products) stay as files. System state summaries are never stored — always generated.

tasks.title: Evaluate OpenClaw for Tier 2 watcher agents
tasks.notes: Persistent intelligent agents for session-history and decisions updates

tasks.title: Explore .claude directory for vault integration
tasks.notes: Investigate transcript files, memories, settings, and other .claude contents. Determine what can be incorporated into the vault system for better context persistence.

tasks.title: Research MCP servers for vault system
tasks.notes: Look into MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers — could provide structured tool access to vault operations, task tracking, and context loading for any Claude interface.

tasks.title: Research sub-agents for vault automation
tasks.notes: Investigate Claude Code sub-agents and Agent SDK for automating vault operations — potential path for Tier 2 watcher agents, context maintenance, and multi-step workflows.

tasks.title: Track chat sessions per project (transcript linkage)
tasks.notes: Track which Claude sessions interacted with each project. Link to specific transcript .jsonl files. Need to solve auto-detection of current session ID. Design: per-project sessions.json or extend event log.

tasks.title: Add provenance tracking to tasks (transcript + message linkage)
tasks.notes: When tasks are created or modified, record which transcript file and ideally which message triggered the change. This creates a traceable chain from task back to the conversation that spawned it. Could store as metadata on each task entry (created_from, modified_from fields) pointing to transcript:message_id pairs.

tasks.title: Transform ML OS demo into agent-facing context pack builder
tasks.notes: CLI-first, not UI-first. Any agent should be able to compose context packs via commands — select kernel, schema, scenario, attach vault sources, stamp with provenance, output a deployable seed prompt. The web UI is secondary. Priority: (1) CLI compose toolchain (2) API endpoints for remote agents (3) Optional web composer. The created agent's context pack should reference the transcript/session where it was designed. Ties to t-fa0b53 (provenance) and t-b7e972 (session tracking).

tasks.title: Design context pack generator — grounding prompt factory for domain-specific agent environments
tasks.notes: Based on ML OS Case Study 001 (Net+ Autonomous Grounding). Generator takes: domain description, intellectual history, canonical state, operator profile, identifiable gap. Outputs: complete .context/ folder with mimir.md, workspace.md, engine file with Phase 0, CLAUDE.md boot sequence. Reference: Runtime Grounding Sequence protocol (Identity Recall > State Ingestion > History Loading > Artifact Contextualization > Gap Identification). Added by cloud agent (Claude Chat) session 2026-03-07.

tasks.title: Revisit: associate Claude Chat App sessions with vault + transcript capture strategy
tasks.notes: Options explored: manual export, session branch metadata (chat URL stored in .session-meta), Cowork browser automation, manual API ping at session start. Core question: do we need transcript content locally or just the association? Defer until clearer. Reference: GAP-002, GAP-008.

tasks.title: Add created_from + agent_id fields to task schema and ingest.py
tasks.notes: Add created_from (session branch or chat URL) and agent_id (e.g. 'cloud-agent', 'code-agent', 'operator') to tasks.json schema. Update ingest.py task add with optional --session and --agent flags. Backward compatible — existing tasks get null for new fields. Closes GAP-002 partially.

tasks.title: Add manual refresh button to vault browser tasks view
tasks.notes: Temp workaround until direct API sync is implemented. Button on tasks page that re-fetches /api/tasks and updates the view. No page reload needed — just re-query and re-render.

tasks.title: Architect direct cloud-agent-to-vault sync via tunnel (Cloudflare/ngrok)
tasks.notes: Current path: cloud agent → GitHub → git pull → local file → browser. 4 hops, manual. Target: expose localhost:3001 via Cloudflare Tunnel or ngrok, cloud agent writes directly to POST /api/tasks, SSE pushes update to browser instantly. Git reverts to its proper role: version control + session branches only, not sync layer.

tasks.title: Build browser extension for real-time Claude Chat transcript capture
tasks.notes: Real-time DOM observer on claude.ai. MutationObserver watches message container — captures {role, text, timestamp, message_id} on each new message node. Logs deletions as events (append-only event log, not a mirror). Native Messaging Host writes to vault/{project}/.session-transcripts/{chat_id}.jsonl — same .jsonl format as Claude Code transcripts so watcher.py can parse both surfaces. Transcript registered in .session-chats.json with surface: claude-chat. Key design decisions: (1) capture on send/receive only not drafts, (2) deletion = logged event not silent drop, (3) timestamps preserved. Closes the cloud agent context gap — without this, Claude Chat sessions leave no local artifact. Session-association model: transcript lives on the session branch under .session-transcripts/, merged to main when session closes.

tasks.title: Add active file watcher to watcher.py for real-time transcript ingestion
tasks.notes:
watcher.py is currently parse-on-demand only — requires manual invocation of 'watcher.py parse <file>'. No active watching loop exists. Fix: add a watchdog daemon mode using Python watchdog library (or polling fallback) that monitors ~/.claude/projects/ for new or modified .jsonl files and auto-parses them into watcher.db as they're written. This is GAP-008. Related: t-9271e6 (browser extension for Claude Chat) is the same problem on a different surface — both should write to the same .jsonl format and downstream pipeline.

## UPDATE 2026-03-08 — Claude Code native cron scheduling now available
Anthropic shipped /loop and CronCreate in Claude Code on 2026-03-07. This changes the implementation approach significantly.

## Preferred implementation: native /loop scheduling
Instead of a custom Python daemon, a Code agent can now self-schedule within a session:
  /loop 1m python3 .mlos/watcher.py parse-all

This fires parse-all every minute while the session is open — no separate process, no watchdog library, no daemon management. The agent maintains its own freshness loop natively.

## Also useful for vault freshness (replaces manual task.sh calls)
  /loop 2m bash .mlos/task.sh list --project mlos-dev --status open

Agent stays current on tasks throughout the session without being told to check.

## Constraint to document
Session-scoped only — tasks die when Claude Code exits. For persistent watching that survives restarts, the OS-level cron (already built in t-d47609) is still required. The two layers complement each other:
  - OS cron (1 min): transcript sync to shared drive — survives restarts
  - /loop in-session: parse-all + task freshness — active while agent is working

## What to build
1. Document the /loop pattern in CLAUDE.md as the recommended in-session freshness mechanism
2. Add a .claude/commands/watch.md skill that agents can invoke to start their freshness loop
3. The Python watchdog daemon approach is now a fallback only — prefer /loop for in-session use

## Acceptance criteria (updated)
1. Agent can run /loop 1m watcher.py parse-all and new transcripts appear in DB within 1 minute
2. watch.md skill documented and available
3. CLAUDE.md updated with /loop freshness pattern
4. OS-level cron (t-d47609) remains as persistent layer — not replaced

tasks.title: Design system for agents to self-modify boot sequences, engines, and CLAUDE.md
tasks.notes: Currently CLAUDE.md, engine files, and boot sequences are manually maintained. Agents notice drift and emergence (flow.md reflexivity protocol) but can't act on it — they can only surface observations to Mimir. Goal: give agents a sanctioned pathway to propose and commit modifications to their own operational context. Design questions: (1) What can an agent modify autonomously vs what requires Mimir approval? (2) How do proposed changes get reviewed before merging — PR model, INBOX proposal, or direct commit with revert option? (3) Which files are in scope: CLAUDE.md, .context/engines/, .context/workspace.md, .context/mimir.md (probably never), .claude/commands/? (4) How does the agent know if its modification worked — what's the feedback loop? (5) How do we prevent drift accumulation where each agent nudges the boot sequence in a slightly different direction? Related: flow.md reflexivity protocol, t-cbeff8 (context rot), GAP-009 (CLAUDE.md files uncoordinated). This is the long-term path to a self-improving workspace.

tasks.title: Figure out what to do with stored Claude Chat transcript URLs
tasks.notes: Two Claude Chat share URLs stored in OpenClaw_Claude/.claude/transcripts/registry.md for session bwgh (2026-03-07). URLs: https://claude.ai/share/ef10710c-f1aa-4035-bc99-f5521c354a13 and https://claude.ai/share/e2be28ae-36c5-47b6-8514-e57cb590b018. Share pages return 403 when fetched programmatically. Questions to resolve: (1) Can Cowork browser agent scrape these while logged in? (2) Should the registry live on the session branch instead of main? (3) What schema should transcript entries follow when t-9271e6 is built — how do URL-based entries migrate to local .jsonl entries? (4) Should INBOX point to registry.md so boot agents know transcripts exist?

tasks.title: Explore Gemini + Google tools integration for large-context and cheap inference use cases
tasks.notes:
Mimir has Google Workspace Pro AI Ultra access (permanent, via beta tester program) and access to Antigrav IDE which has proprietary plugins for Google AI APIs including NotebookLM. Key insight: Gemini's primary advantage is massive context window + needle-in-a-haystack retrieval at low cost. This makes it a natural complement to Claude for specific use cases, not a replacement.

## Identified use cases
1. Large-context tasks on the cheap — feed entire vault, transcript history, engine files as context. Gemini handles the retrieval, Claude handles the reasoning and output.
2. NotebookLM as queryable knowledge base — periodically sync vault docs and transcripts into a NotebookLM notebook. Agents query it for historical context instead of loading everything into the window.
3. Google Drive as shared drive redundancy — Z: going down was a single point of failure. Drive doesn't have that problem.
4. Antigrav IDE — investigate what Google APIs are scriptable vs GUI-only. If scriptable, map to current system gaps.

## Design principle
Do NOT redesign the coordination layer (git, vault, INBOX, session branches) for Google tools. Google tools slot INTO that layer as consumers or contributors — source of truth stays in git/vault.

## Questions to answer
1. What does Antigrav actually expose programmatically? API access or GUI only?
2. Can NotebookLM be fed programmatically (Drive sync, API) or manual upload only?
3. Which current open tasks could be partially closed by Google tools? (t-889a68 transcript capture, t-11cc79 transcript storage, t-cbeff8 context rot)
4. What's the cost model — is Gemini API free under Workspace Ultra or separate billing?

tasks.title: Implement per-turn repo freshness check across all agents
tasks.notes:
Each agent operates on whatever state they pulled at boot. No mechanism exists to detect if another agent pushed something mid-session. This causes stale reads — an agent acts on outdated task state, vault data, or INBOX without knowing it.

## The pattern: dirty flag check on every turn
Before responding each turn, agent checks if remote is ahead of local. If yes, pull before proceeding. Check must be cheap — not a full pull, just a lightweight probe.

## Recommended implementation: GitHub API commit hash check
Each agent tracks the last commit hash it pulled. On every turn:
  GET https://api.github.com/repos/mrmeman555/Claude-Cowork-Vault/commits?per_page=1
  GET https://api.github.com/repos/mrmeman555/OpenClaw_Claude/commits?per_page=1
Compare returned sha against last-known sha. If different — pull, log it, then proceed.
One API call per repo per turn. Fast, no extra infrastructure, works from any surface.

## What needs to be built
1. Standard preamble protocol — document the check as a required step in CLAUDE.md and engine files. Agents that can run git do a pull. Cloud agents (Claude Chat) note the drift and flag it.
2. Update /sync command — already does a freshness check, but only on demand. This makes it continuous.
3. Store last-known sha — agents need somewhere to track this. Options: in-memory per session (simplest), or written to a local state file.
4. Acknowledgement pattern — when an agent pulls mid-session, it should briefly note what changed before continuing. Not verbose, just: 'Pulled — 2 new commits on Claude-Cowork-Vault (task updates).'

## Scope
- Claude Code agents: run git pull directly
- Claude Chat agents (this surface): cannot pull directly, but can hit GitHub API and flag drift to operator
- Bootstrap: already pulls at session start — this closes the mid-session gap

## Relation to existing tasks
- Extends /sync command (already in .claude/commands/)
- Complements t-47b4df (active watcher) — watcher handles local file changes, this handles remote changes
- Should be documented in CLAUDE.md as part of boot sequence Step 0

tasks.title: Centralize Claude Code transcripts from all devices to shared drive for unified watcher ingestion
tasks.notes:
IMPLEMENT IMMEDIATELY. Highest priority build task.

## Why urgent
Without this, every agent is blind to what every other agent is doing in real time. Transcripts sit on the machine that produced them and go nowhere. Goal: near-real-time transcript sharing across all devices via shared drive so any agent can read any other agent's live session.

## Device inventory (confirmed)
- device-1: Windows (primary), shared drive at Z:/Projects/ML_OS/
- device-2: Linux (aaron-ROG), shared drive at /mnt/share/Projects/ML_OS/

## Step 1 — Create target folder structure on shared drive
  mkdir -p /mnt/share/Projects/ML_OS/transcripts/device-1
  mkdir -p /mnt/share/Projects/ML_OS/transcripts/device-2

## Step 2 — Device 1 (Windows): scheduled robocopy every 1 minute
  schtasks /create /tn "MLOS-TranscriptSync" /tr "robocopy %USERPROFILE%\.claude\projects Z:\Projects\ML_OS\transcripts\device-1 *.jsonl /E /XO /R:1 /W:1" /sc minute /mo 1 /f
  Verify: Z:\Projects\ML_OS\transcripts\device-1\ should have .jsonl files within 1 minute.

## Step 3 — Device 2 (Linux): cron job every 1 minute
  Add to crontab (crontab -e):
    * * * * * rsync -av --ignore-existing ~/.claude/projects/ /mnt/share/Projects/ML_OS/transcripts/device-2/ --include="*.jsonl" --include="*/" --exclude="*" 2>/dev/null

  Or for true real-time using inotifywait:
    sudo apt-get install inotify-tools
    Create ~/.mlos/transcript-sync.sh:
      #!/bin/bash
      while inotifywait -r -e close_write ~/.claude/projects/; do
        rsync -av --ignore-existing ~/.claude/projects/ /mnt/share/Projects/ML_OS/transcripts/device-2/ --include="*.jsonl" --include="*/" --exclude="*"
      done

## Step 4 — Update watcher.py to watch shared drive transcript store
In Home_Lab_2026/.mlos/watcher.py add both device folders as watched paths:
  /mnt/share/Projects/ML_OS/transcripts/device-1/
  /mnt/share/Projects/ML_OS/transcripts/device-2/
Tag ingested transcripts with source device for provenance.

## Step 5 — Add sync step to session-close.sh
Before git operations add:
  rsync -av --ignore-existing ~/.claude/projects/ /mnt/share/Projects/ML_OS/transcripts/device-2/ --include="*.jsonl" --include="*/" --exclude="*"

## Acceptance criteria
1. Claude Code session on device-1 -> within 1 min, .jsonl appears in transcripts/device-1/
2. Claude Code session on device-2 -> within 1 min, .jsonl appears in transcripts/device-2/
3. watcher.py parse on a shared drive transcript file succeeds
4. Device-1 scheduled task survives reboot
5. Device-2 cron survives reboot

## What NOT to do
- Do not move files, copy only — originals stay in ~/.claude/projects/
- Do not change .jsonl format — watcher.py already parses it
- Do not use Z: paths on Linux — use /mnt/share/ equivalents

tasks.title: Agent booted with stale vault — Claude-Cowork-Vault pull not happening at boot
tasks.notes: Observed 2026-03-08: Linux agent (device-2) booted from OpenClaw_Claude but read stale Claude-Cowork-Vault tasks. Could not find t-d47609 which had been pushed. Root cause: CLAUDE.md Step 0d runs ingest.py task list but does not first pull Claude-Cowork-Vault. The agent pulls OpenClaw_Claude at boot but not the vault repo — they are two separate repos. Fix: add explicit git pull step for Claude-Cowork-Vault to CLAUDE.md Step 0, before the task query. Command to add: cd $MLOS_ROOT/Home_Lab_2026 && git pull origin main. This is a concrete instance of the problem t-4e6704 describes (per-turn freshness check). For now the immediate fix is just adding the vault pull to Step 0d in CLAUDE.md.

tasks.title: SQLite watcher.db locking on shared drive — needs per-device local DB + merge layer
tasks.notes: Hit during t-d47609 implementation: watcher.py uses a single watcher.db on the shared drive. When two devices run parse-all simultaneously, SQLite locks. Current workaround: WATCHER_DB=/tmp/local.db env var. Proper solution: each device maintains its own local DB (e.g. ~/.mlos/watcher.db), and watcher.py has a read layer that can query across both when doing sessions/activity queries. OR use WAL mode (sqlite journal_mode=WAL) which handles concurrent readers better — simpler fix. Evaluate WAL first, fall back to per-device DB if still problematic.

tasks.title: Custom git merge driver for events.jsonl and tasks.json — stop recurring rebase conflicts
tasks.notes:
Root cause: both agents append to events.jsonl and tasks.json independently. Git sees two diverged versions and conflicts every time. Current workaround: agent takes --theirs, which silently drops local changes. This is dangerous for an events log. Fix: implement a custom git merge driver that knows the semantics of each file. For events.jsonl: merge driver should concatenate all entries from both sides, deduplicate by event ID/timestamp, and sort chronologically. For tasks.json: merge driver should merge by task ID — if same task modified on both sides, take the newer updated timestamp. Implementation: 1) Write a Python merge script (.mlos/merge-driver.py) that handles both files. 2) Register in .gitattributes: .mlos/events.jsonl merge=mlos-append and vault/mlos-dev/tasks.json merge=mlos-tasks. 3) Register driver in .git/config: [merge 'mlos-append'] driver = python3 .mlos/merge-driver.py %O %A %B. This eliminates the recurring conflict entirely without requiring agent coordination.

## Implementation (complete)
- .mlos/merge-driver.py — Python merge script, handles both file types
- .gitattributes — registers driver for events.jsonl and tasks.json

## Each device must run once to register the driver in local git config:
  cd /path/to/Home_Lab_2026
  git config merge.mlos-events.driver "python3 .mlos/merge-driver.py %O %A %B events"
  git config merge.mlos-events.name "ML OS events.jsonl merge driver"
  git config merge.mlos-tasks.driver "python3 .mlos/merge-driver.py %O %A %B tasks"
  git config merge.mlos-tasks.name "ML OS tasks.json merge driver"

Run this on BOTH device-1 and device-2. The .gitattributes is committed
so git knows which files use which driver — but the driver itself must be
registered in each device's local .git/config (not committed).

COMPLETED 2026-03-08. merge-driver.py + .gitattributes deployed by cloud agent. Tested by code agent: local t-test01 vs remote t-test02 conflict resolved cleanly. Fixed %%O escaping for Linux. Windows git config needs single-% fix (documented).

tasks.title: Add completion_steps field to task schema — enforce commit+push+close on every task
tasks.notes: Problem: agents complete implementation work but don't commit, push, or mark tasks done. They treat 'working locally' as done. Fix: add a mandatory completion_steps block to the task notes template. Every task that touches files must include: git add -A && git commit -m 'feat: t-XXXX — description' && git push && python3 .mlos/ingest.py task update --id t-XXXX --status done. Also update CLAUDE.md task execution protocol to say: a task is not done until committed, pushed, and marked done in vault. Consider adding completion_steps as a first-class field in tasks.json schema so ingest.py can surface it automatically when an agent picks up a task.

tasks.title: Build task.sh wrapper — enforce pull-operate-push on every vault interaction
tasks.notes:
Core problem: agents treat GitHub as a backup, not the source of truth. They read stale local state and write without pushing. Fix: a wrapper script that enforces the write-through protocol on every task operation.

## What to build: .mlos/task.sh

READ usage:
  bash .mlos/task.sh list [--project mlos-dev]
  -> git pull origin main
  -> python3 .mlos/ingest.py task list ...
  -> outputs result

WRITE usage:
  bash .mlos/task.sh add --title '...' --priority high ...
  bash .mlos/task.sh update --id t-xxxx --status done
  -> git pull origin main
  -> python3 .mlos/ingest.py task add/update ...
  -> git add -A && git commit -m 'task: auto-commit from task.sh'
  -> git push origin main

## Rules
- task.sh ALWAYS pulls before any operation
- task.sh ALWAYS pushes after any write
- Agents NEVER call ingest.py directly for task operations
- If push fails, retry pull+push once before erroring

## CLAUDE.md update
Replace all ingest.py task references with task.sh equivalents. Add to protocol: 'Never call ingest.py task commands directly. Always use .mlos/task.sh.'

## Acceptance criteria
1. task.sh list always returns fresh data (verify with two agents writing simultaneously)
2. task.sh add creates task AND pushes in one command
3. task.sh update marks done AND pushes in one command
4. No manual git add/commit/push needed after any task operation
5. CLAUDE.md updated to reference task.sh

## On completion
git add -A && git commit -m 'feat: task.sh write-through wrapper' && git push
python3 .mlos/ingest.py task update --id <this-task-id> --status done

tasks.title: Document two valid working modes — session workflow vs direct-to-main
tasks.notes:
Two valid working modes exist. Both are intentional. CLAUDE.md should surface this so agents don't assume one is broken.

## Mode 1: Session Workflow
Use when: structured work, clear scope, multiple agents coordinating on a feature, want clean handoff notes.
Tools: bootstrap.sh (create session branch), session-close.sh (merge, write INBOX, clean up).
Branch pattern: session/YYYY-MM-DD-topic
Handoff: INBOX.md entry summarizing what happened.

## Mode 2: Direct-to-Main (current default)
Use when: exploratory work, rapid iteration, operator present, tasks are short-lived.
Tools: task.sh for all task ops, transcript sync handles continuity, merge driver handles conflicts.
Branch pattern: none — work directly on main.
Handoff: transcripts on shared drive, tasks in vault.

## What to do
Update CLAUDE.md to document both modes at the top level. Agents should detect which mode is active (check current branch — if session/* then session mode, if main then direct mode) and behave accordingly. Neither mode is wrong.

tasks.title: Build custom Claude chat interface — unified frontend with vault knowledge backend
tasks.notes:
Core idea: replace claude.ai web app with a custom chat interface built around ML OS infrastructure. The web app and Claude Code share a usage pool anyway — there is no cost reason to use the web app. The reason to build this is capability, not cost.

## What it needs to do that claude.ai does not
- Artifact display and management
- Context management panel — explicitly control what context is in each conversation
- Sidebar panels: open tasks, vault state, active sessions on other devices, transcript viewer
- Full access to ALL prior conversations as a knowledge source (not just current session)
- Semantic search over transcript corpus (RAG over your own history)

## Layout (IDE-first approach)
Three-panel layout inside Antigravity IDE:
  LEFT:   Chat interface + artifact rendering + context manager + task sidebar
  CENTER: Browser panel (vault browser, web preview, file tree, transcript viewer) — resizable
  RIGHT:  Terminal (Claude Code, command output)

Starting in IDE rather than standalone app — lower friction, terminal already there, no shipping required. Browser panel is not just a web browser — it is vault browser, artifact rendering, local file preview, all in one resizable pane.

## Context management panel — live knowledge graph
Core feature: as a conversation progresses, a graph visualization shows all nodes and edges related to the current context. Nodes are tasks, transcripts, decisions, commits. Edges are relationships between them — task ID references, topic overlap, shared entities.

The graph assembles itself from the enrichment layer (t-8dd083) and task ID references found in messages. As the conversation progresses new edges form in real time. You mention a task ID, the graph expands. You reference a concept matching prior transcripts, those nodes surface.

This makes context management visual and intuitive — you see what is relevant rather than having to decide. Dense cluster of nodes = that is where the history lives. The graph IS the context manager.

Example: discussing t-2a58df surfaces edges to t-9271e6, t-11cc79, t-fa0b53, t-8dd083 and all transcript sessions that ever mentioned those IDs. Fully assembled without any manual curation.

## Architecture

### The two-layer principle (critical)
Git and SQLite are NOT redundant. They serve fundamentally different purposes and both are essential.

GIT = what happened, who did it, when, in what order. Immutable history. The truth. Provenance.
SQLITE = what does current state look like, what is searchable, what is relevant right now. Derived from git. Rebuildable from scratch at any time by re-running parse-all.

If SQLite gets corrupted or out of sync — rebuild from git history. You never lose anything because the source of truth is the repo, not the database. SQLite is a read optimization, not a replacement.

### Why git provenance matters
Every task creation, every decision, every agent action has a commit hash, a timestamp, an author. That is provenance you cannot get from a database write. The full chain becomes queryable:
  'Mimir said X in a conversation' → agent created task → agent implemented it → commit hash
This is what t-fa0b53 (provenance tracking) is building toward. In this architecture it becomes fully realized.

### Write path (unchanged)
  Agents → git commit/push → GitHub (source of truth for tasks, decisions, structured state)

### Ingest path (already built)
  OS cron (1min) → rsync transcripts to shared drive
  watcher.py parse-all → SQLite watcher.db (shared drive)

### Read path (to build)
  SQLite watcher.db → small query API server → chat frontend + agents
  Endpoints needed:
    GET /search?q=<query>        — full-text search over transcript corpus
    GET /context?topic=<topic>   — retrieve relevant prior context for injection
    GET /tasks?status=open       — live task state
    GET /sessions                — recent sessions across all devices
    GET /graph?anchor=<task_id>  — return node/edge graph centered on a task or topic

### Frontend (to build)
  Chat interface wrapping Anthropic API directly
  Artifact rendering panel
  Live knowledge graph — context manager panel (see above)
  Sidebar: tasks, vault state, transcript viewer, active device sessions

## Build order
1. Message enrichment pipeline (t-8dd083) — creates the edges the graph needs
2. Graph assembly in SQLite — nodes and edges stored as query results
3. Query API with /graph endpoint
4. Graph visualization panel in the IDE interface
5. Full chat interface wrapping everything

## MVP scope
1. Query API server (Flask or FastAPI) reading from watcher.db
2. Basic chat UI hitting Anthropic API with context injection from query API
3. Task sidebar panel (live, reads from API not git)
4. Live knowledge graph panel as context manager

## Relationship to existing tasks
- t-cbeff8 (context-rotting files) — this makes it irrelevant, dynamic context replaces static files
- t-47b4df (active watcher) — feeds the SQLite layer this depends on
- t-50fea1 (context pack generator) — becomes a feature of the context manager panel
- t-49c238 (cloud-agent-to-vault sync) — the query API is the sync mechanism
- t-fa0b53 (provenance tracking) — fully realized in this architecture via git commit chain
- t-8dd083 (message enrichment) — creates the node/edge data the graph visualizes
- t-9271e6 (browser extension) — temporary bridge, less critical once custom interface exists

## This is a long-horizon project — design before building
Do not start implementation until architecture is validated. Key open questions:
- Embedding model for semantic search (local vs API)
- Auth model for the query API (localhost only vs tunneled for cloud access)
- How context injection works in practice (automatic vs manual selection)
- Graph rendering library (D3, Cytoscape, or similar)

tasks.title: Inferred project knowledge — AI querying transcript corpus to reconstruct intent and context
tasks.notes:
Core insight from brainstorming session 2026-03-08:

The transcript corpus is not documentation — it is something more valuable. Documentation is lossy compression written by someone who already understood the thing. Transcripts are the raw record of how the system came to be.

## What this enables
Any agent (or human) can ask specific questions about any project and the AI can query the transcript corpus + git history to reconstruct:
- Why a decision was made (not just what was decided)
- What alternatives were considered and rejected
- What constraints existed at the time
- What was tried before and why it failed
- The intent behind any piece of code or architecture

This is qualitatively different from any documentation system that has ever existed. Documentation requires someone to decide what is worth writing down. Transcripts capture everything including the things nobody thought were worth capturing — which are often the most important things.

## The compounding effect
The longer the system runs, the more valuable it gets. Six months of transcripts gives any new agent more institutional knowledge about ML OS than most engineers have about systems they have worked on for years.

Most knowledge systems decay — documentation goes stale, wikis get abandoned, people leave. This system does the opposite. Every session adds signal. The corpus only gets denser.

## The provenance chain makes it trustworthy
Not 'here is what someone wrote about how this works.' Instead: here is the commit, here is the conversation that produced it, here is the constraint that shaped it, here is what was tried before. Full trace from current state back to the moment of decision.

## What needs to be built to realize this
1. Semantic search over transcript corpus (embedding model — see t-2a58df)
2. Query API that accepts natural language questions and returns relevant transcript segments
3. Context injection into agent boot sequences — agents start sessions already knowing relevant history
4. Provenance linking — tasks and commits linked to the transcript messages that created them (t-fa0b53)

## This is unprecedented
No existing system does this without very specifically designed and maintained project documentation. This emerges as a side effect of just working with the transcript capture infrastructure already built.

tasks.title: Message enrichment pipeline — structured extraction from transcript messages into queryable atomic notes
tasks.notes:
Core idea from brainstorming session 2026-03-08:

Every ingested message could pass through an enrichment step that extracts structured metadata alongside the raw transcript. Messages become highly accessible atomic notes — decisions, questions, hypotheses, action items, entity references — all queryable without needing to read full conversation prose.

## How it works
1. watcher.py ingests transcript message as-is (unchanged)
2. Enrichment step runs on each new message:
   - Pass message + surrounding context to a small fast model (Haiku)
   - Structured output: JSON with extracted fields
   - Store enrichment in separate DB table alongside original message
3. Query layer can search enrichment metadata, then return original message for full context

## Enrichment fields to extract (initial set)
- type: decision | question | hypothesis | action_item | observation | reference
- entities: task IDs, file paths, system names, people mentioned
- tags: inferred topic tags
- confidence: how certain the extraction is
- summary: 1-sentence summary of the message
- links_to: references to other messages, commits, tasks

## Key properties
- Original transcript is NEVER modified — enrichment is additive only
- Retroactive — can run enrichment over entire existing corpus
- The transcript stays as the source of truth, enrichment is derived
- Small fast model keeps cost low (Haiku at fraction of Opus cost)

## Why this is powerful
Transforms conversational prose into a structured knowledge graph without requiring anyone to deliberately take notes. Every message becomes a node. Relationships between messages, tasks, decisions, and code emerge automatically.

Combines with t-88e7bb (inferred project knowledge) and t-2a58df (custom chat interface) to form the full knowledge layer.
